lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Thou, nature, art my goddess; to thy law

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: My services are bound. Wherefore should I

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Stand in the plague of custom, and permit

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: The curiosity of nations to deprive me,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: For that I am some twelve or fourteen moon-shines

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Lag of a brother? Why bastard? wherefore base?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: When my dimensions are as well compact,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: My mind as generous, and my shape as true,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: As honest madam’s issue? Why brand they us

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: With base? with baseness? bastardy? base, base?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Who, in the lusty stealth of nature, take

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: More composition and fierce quality

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Than doth, within a dull, stale, tired bed,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Go to the creating a whole tribe of fops,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Got ’tween asleep and wake? Well, then,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Legitimate Edgar, I must have your land:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Our father’s love is to the bastard Edmund

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: As to the legitimate: fine word—legitimate!

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Well, my legitimate, if this letter speed,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: And my invention thrive, Edmund the base

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Shall top the legitimate. I grow; I prosper:

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Now, gods, stand up for bastards!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Kent banish’d thus! and France in choler parted!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And the king gone to-night! subscribed his power!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Confined to exhibition! All this done

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Upon the gad! Edmund, how now! what news?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: So please your lordship, none. Putting up the letter.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Why so earnestly seek you to put up that letter?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I know no news, my lord.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What paper were you reading?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Nothing, my lord.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No? What needed, then, that terrible dispatch of it into your pocket?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: the quality of nothing hath not such need to hide itself. Let’s

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: see: come, if it be nothing, I shall not need spectacles.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I beseech you, sir, pardon me: it is a letter from my

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: brother, that I have not all o’er-read; and for so much as

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I have perused, I find it not fit for your o’er-looking.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Give me the letter, sir.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I shall offend, either to detain or give it. The contents, as

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: in part I understand them, are to blame.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Let’s see, let’s see.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I hope, for my brother’s justification, he wrote this but as an

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: essay or taste of my virtue.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: “This policy and reverence of age makes the world bitter to the

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: best of our times; keeps our fortunes from us till our oldness

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: cannot relish them. I begin to find an idle and fond bondage

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: in the oppression of aged tyranny; who sways, not as it hath

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: power, but as it is suffered. Come to me, that of this

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I may speak more. If our father would sleep till I waked

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: him, you should half his revenue for ever, and live the beloved

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: of your brother, Edgar.”

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: It was not brought me, my lord; there’s the cunning of it;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I found it thrown in at the casement of my closet.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You know the character to be your brother’s?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: If the matter were good, my lord, I durst swear it were

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: his; but, in respect of that, I would fain think it were

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: not.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: It is his.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: It is his hand, my lord; but I hope his heart is

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: not in the contents.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Hath he never heretofore sounded you in this business?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Never, my lord: but I have heard him oft maintain it to

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: be fit, that, sons at perfect age, and fathers declining, the father

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: should be as ward to the son, and the son manage his

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: revenue.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O villain, villain! His very opinion in the letter! Abhorred villain! Unnatural,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: detested, brutish villain! worse than brutish! Go, sirrah, seek him; I’ll apprehend

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: him: abominable villain! Where is he?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I do not well know, my lord. If it shall please you

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: to suspend your indignation against my brother till you can derive from

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: him better testimony of his intent, you shall run a certain course;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: where, if you violently proceed against him, mistaking his purpose, it would

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: make a great gap in your own honour, and shake in pieces

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: the heart of his obedience. I dare pawn down my life for

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: him, that he hath wrote this to feel my affection to your

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: honour, and to no further pretence of danger.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Think you so?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: If your honour judge it meet, I will place you where you

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: shall hear us confer of this, and by an auricular assurance have

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: your satisfaction; and that without any further delay than this very evening.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He cannot be such a monster—

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Nor is not, sure.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To his father, that so tenderly and entirely loves him. Heaven and

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: earth! Edmund, seek him out: wind me into him, I pray you:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: frame the business after your own wisdom. I would unstate myself, to

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: be in a due resolution.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I will seek him, sir, presently: convey the business as I shall

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: find means and acquaint you withal.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: These late eclipses in the sun and moon portend no good to

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: us: though the wisdom of nature can reason it thus and thus,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: yet nature finds itself scourged by the sequent effects: love cools, friendship

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: falls off, brothers divide: in cities, mutinies; in countries, discord; in palaces,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: treason; and the bond cracked ’twixt son and father. This villain of

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: mine comes under the prediction; there’s son against father: the king falls

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: from bias of nature; there’s father against child. We have seen the

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: best of our time: machinations, hollowness, treachery, and all ruinous disorders, follow

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: us disquietly to our graves. Find out this villain, Edmund; it shall

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: lose thee nothing; do it carefully. And the noble and true-hearted Kent

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: banished! his offence, honesty! ’Tis strange.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: This is the excellent foppery of the world, that, when we are

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: sick in fortune—often the surfeit of our own behavior—we make guilty of

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: our disasters the sun, the moon, and the stars: as if we

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: were villains by necessity; fools by heavenly compulsion; knaves, thieves, and treachers,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: by spherical predominance; drunkards, liars, and adulterers, by an enforced obedience of

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: planetary influence; and all that we are evil in, by a divine

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: thrusting on: an admirable evasion of whoremaster man, to lay his goatish

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: disposition to the charge of a star! My father compounded with my

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: mother under the dragon’s tail; and my nativity was under Ursa major;

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: so that it follows, I am rough and lecherous. Tut, I should

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: have been that I am, had the maidenliest star in the firmament

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: twinkled on my bastardizing. Edgar—

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: How now, brother Edmund! what serious contemplation are you in?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I am thinking, brother, of a prediction I read this other day,

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: what should follow these eclipses.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Do you busy yourself about that?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I promise you, the effects he writes of succeed unhappily; as of

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: unnaturalness between the child and the parent; death, dearth, dissolutions of ancient

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: amities; divisions in state, menaces and maledictions against king and nobles; needless

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: diffidences, banishment of friends, dissipation of cohorts, nuptial breaches, and I know

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: not what.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: How long have you been a sectary astronomical?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Come, come; when saw you my father last?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Why, the night gone by.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Spake you with him?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Ay, two hours together.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Parted you in good terms? Found you no displeasure in him by word or countenance?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: None at all.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Bethink yourself wherein you may have offended him: and at my entreaty

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: forbear his presence till some little time hath qualified the heat of

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: his displeasure; which at this instant so rageth in him, that with

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: the mischief of your person it would scarcely allay.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Some villain hath done me wrong.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: That’s my fear. I pray you, have a continent forbearance till the

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: spied of his rage goes slower; and, as I say, retire with

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: me to my lodging, from whence I will fitly bring you to

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: hear my lord speak: pray ye, go; there’s my key: if you

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: do stir abroad, go armed.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Armed, brother!

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Brother, I advise you to the best; go armed: I am no

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: honest man if there be any good meaning towards you: I have

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: told you what I have seen and heard; but faintly, nothing like

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: the image and horror of it: pray you, away.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Shall I hear from you anon?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: I do serve you in this business.

stage_directions.text: Hum—conspiracy!—“Sleep till I waked him—you should enjoy half his revenue,”—My son Edgar! Had he a hand to write this? a heart and brain to breed it in?—When came this to you? who brought it?
And pat he comes like the catastrophe of the old comedy: my cue is villainous melancholy, with a sigh like Tom o’ Bedlam. O, these eclipses do portend these divisions! fa, sol, la, mi.

A credulous father! and a brother noble, Whose nature is so far from doing harms, That he suspects none: on whose foolish honesty My practises ride easy! I see the business. Let me, if not by birth, have lands by wit: All with me’s meet that I can fashion fit. Exit.

glosses.definition: Edmund's deity — the principle of natural force, opposed to law and custom; the speech that defines his character
the curse of tradition; social conventions Edmund despises
the nice distinctions societies draw (legitimate / illegitimate)
illegitimate child — Edmund seizes the word as his self-definition
vigorous, secret sexual energy of natural desire
boring marital sex — Edmund prefers illicit passion
Edgar, the lawful heir; Edmund's foil
the illegitimate shall surpass the legitimate
right on cue — Edmund sees Edgar approach
prudent statecraft — here with a sinister edge
income; what Gloucester would leave Edgar
lazy and foolish obligation — to fathers
folly of the age — Edmund's scornful view of astrology
astrology — which Edmund rejects as alibi
astrological influence of a dominant planet
the descending node of the moon's orbit — astrological term
madman — Bedlam (Bethlem Hospital, London) was the notorious asylum
the real eclipses of 1605; Gloucester's astrological alarm

glosses.phrase: to no further pretence of danger
glosses.definition: no further apparent danger — Edmund pretending to protect Edgar
evidence of the ears; firsthand hearing
recent eclipses — the 1605 eclipses Gloucester reads as portents
literal-biological (as opposed to legitimate) — Edmund's pun on his status
my role's line — theatrical metaphor for his cold planning

annotations.title: Edmund's 'Nature' soliloquy — the play's materialist manifesto
annotations.body: Edmund's opening speech is the most dangerous in the play: a young man invoking 'Nature' against custom and law, claiming that illegitimate sons have 'more composition and fierce quality' than children 'got 'tween asleep and wake' in tired marital beds. This is Renaissance anti-humanism avant la lettre — Machiavellian in content, Senecan in rhetoric. Edmund has no supernatural explanation for his resentment and does not want one; he later rejects astrology as 'the excellent foppery of the world.' He is the play's first thoroughgoing modern — self-made, unbelieving, free of metaphysical scruple. Shakespeare lets him speak this speech with such rhetorical force that audiences often find themselves agreeing before they remember what Edmund will do. That seduction is deliberate: the play is testing how much of traditional morality survives when someone refuses to take it seriously.

annotations.title: Gloucester on the eclipses — astrological anxiety vs. Edmund's scorn
annotations.body: Gloucester's catalogue of dangers following the recent eclipses ('unnaturalness between the child and the parent; death, dearth, dissolutions of ancient amities; divisions in state...') is a contemporary anxiety Shakespeare's first audience recognised — eclipses in October 1605 had set off a pamphlet industry of catastrophic predictions. Gloucester speaks in the voice of the credulous early-modern: Edmund rebuts him silently in the soliloquy that follows. The play does not choose between them. The catalogue turns out to be accurate — families do break, kingdoms do divide — but not because of the stars. Shakespeare lets Gloucester be right about effects and wrong about causes, which is the play's most efficient dramatic-ironic stance.
sources: Thomas Dekker, The Belman of London (1608)
F. P. Wilson, Elizabethan and Jacobean (1945)

trials.prompt: What does Edmund mean by invoking 'Nature' as his goddess?
options: He rejects custom, law, and legitimacy as arbitrary conventions, claiming natural vigor over social categories — a materialist manifesto
He is a pagan religious believer

trials.prompt: What is Edmund's view of astrology (lines ~103–120)?
options: He dismisses it as 'excellent foppery' — a self-excusing fiction for moral weakness
He accepts only certain forms of it

trials.prompt: Why would Gloucester's astrological speech have resonated with Shakespeare's first audience?
options: It didn't resonate — audiences ignored it
Real eclipses in October 1605 had triggered a pamphlet industry of doom predictions; Gloucester's catalogue speaks in the contemporary credulous register
